By age 30, he reported resolving most early trauma and achieving a foundational sense of stability, though societal trauma exposure continued to cause suffering. The author proposes reconceptualising psychedelics as tools for understanding ordinary mental states rather than pathological ones.
clinical_insight: This case is notable for its detailed documentation of a self-directed, multi-year psychedelic process — a reality many clinicians will encounter as patients increasingly present with unsupervised psychedelic use histories. The key clinical insight is the patient own discovery that gradual dose reduction combined with sustained somatic focus produced better results than occasional high-dose sessions, directly challenging the prevailing clinical trial model of single or double high-dose administration. The case also illustrates how iatrogenic harm from psychiatric services (experienced rejection) can push patients toward self-treatment, raising questions about how clinicians receive and respond to patients psychedelic experiences. A significant limitation is the retrospective, self-reported nature of the data, and the absence of standardised outcome measures.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists whose patients have used or are considering using psychedelics for self-treatment of C-PTSD and treatment-resistant depression

title: Qualitative Analysis of a Patient Experience of Ketamine-Assisted Psychotherapy for Substance Use Disorder: Empirical Synergies with Twelve-Step Programs
summary: This case report details a 28-year-old woman treated for alcohol and prescription opioid use disorder using an inter-clinic collaborative treatment plan combining ketamine-assisted psychotherapy (KAP) with existing 12-step-based psychosocial treatments. Qualitative analysis of the patient lived experience revealed that ketamine-induced experiences meaningfully overlapped with 12-step concepts such as powerlessness, spiritual awakening, and moral inventory. The authors detail specific clinical practices for managing the unique risks of using ketamine — itself a substance with recreational potential — as a treatment for SUD, and highlight the synergies between psychedelic experiences and 12-step recovery frameworks.
clinical_insight: As ketamine clinics expand rapidly, the question of how to integrate KAP with established addiction treatment frameworks like 12-step programs is increasingly urgent. This case report provides a practical clinical roadmap: rather than positioning KAP as an alternative to 12-step work, the authors demonstrate how ketamine experiences can deepen engagement with recovery concepts such as surrender, spiritual experience, and self-examination. For addiction clinicians, the detailed risk-management practices for using a psychoactive substance to treat substance use disorder are particularly valuable. The case highlights a tension in the field: patients may attribute recovery to the ketamine experience itself, while clinicians may see it as a catalyst for engagement with existing therapeutic frameworks. A limitation is the single-case design and the specific cultural context of 12-step programs, which may not generalise to all treatment approaches.
relevant_for: Addiction clinicians integrating ketamine-assisted psychotherapy with 12-step or other structured psychosocial treatment programs

title: MDMA-Assisted Therapy for Complex-Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder: Toward a Neurocognitive Account
summary: This paper proposes a novel neurocognitive model explaining how MDMA-assisted therapy may specifically benefit individuals with complex PTSD (C-PTSD). Drawing on active inference and self-modelling theories, the authors elucidate the insula’s role in affective regulation and how its dysfunction underpins C-PTSD symptoms including affective dysregulation, negative self-concept, and interpersonal difficulties. The framework suggests MDMA may recalibrate affective regulation and reinforce a more stable self-model, distinguishing its therapeutic potential for C-PTSD from standard PTSD approaches.
clinical_insight: Clinicians working with C-PTSD patients should note the proposed distinction between standard PTSD (fear-conditioning based) and C-PTSD (self-model disruption). The insula-centric framework offers a rationale for why C-PTSD patients may benefit from MDMA’s prosocial and emotion-regulating effects beyond simple fear extinction. A notable blind spot: the model is purely theoretical and has not been empirically validated in C-PTSD populations specifically, so clinical application should await trial data.
relevant_for: Clinicians specializing in trauma therapy, particularly those working with patients who have prolonged interpersonal trauma histories and exhibit disturbances in self-organization

title: Exploring Psilocybin-Assisted Schema Therapy: A Conceptual Framework for Potential Therapeutic Synergies in Personality Disorders
summary: This conceptual paper proposes a novel integration of psilocybin-assisted therapy with Schema Therapy (PAST) for personality disorders. Within a predictive coding framework, the authors draw parallels between Early Maladaptive Schemas and high-level priors, arguing that psilocybin’s ability to relax entrenched beliefs (per the REBUS/REBAS models) may enhance Schema Therapy’s experiential and cognitive interventions. The model suggests combining psilocybin sessions with ST integration work to strengthen adaptive modes and reduce dysfunctional schema modes, potentially reducing treatment duration for Cluster B and C personality disorders.
clinical_insight: Schema therapists working with personality-disordered patients may find the predictive coding rationale useful for conceptualizing why some patients show rigid resistance to schema mode shifts. The proposed PAST model offers a theoretical justification for combining psychedelic-facilitated cognitive flexibility with established ST techniques. However, the authors appropriately caution that this remains conceptual—no clinical data yet support psilocybin use in PD populations, and the potential for destabilization in borderline patients requires careful consideration.
relevant_for: Schema therapists and clinicians working with personality disorders who are following developments in psychedelic-assisted psychotherapy

title: Day-to-Day Dynamics of Facial Emotion Expressions in Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
summary: This innovative study applied idiographic (person-specific) network modeling to daily facial emotion expressions and PTSD symptoms in 112 World Trade Center responders over 90 days, yielding 8,953 video diary recordings analyzed with facial emotion recognition. Eighty percent of participants showed at least one reliable expression-symptom link, with significant heterogeneity—some individuals’ symptoms related to emotional over-expressivity while others showed under-expressivity. The study moves beyond self-report to capture outward emotional behavior as a mechanism maintaining PTSD.
clinical_insight: The finding that PTSD emotion-symptom dynamics are highly individualized has direct implications for case conceptualization: clinicians cannot assume a uniform emotional presentation in PTSD. Some patients may benefit from interventions targeting emotional expression (e.g., exposure-based approaches), while others may need help amplifying emotional awareness. The study’s strength lies in its ecological validity and idiographic approach, though the WTC responder sample limits generalizability to other trauma populations such as interpersonal trauma survivors.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating PTSD, particularly those interested in personalized case formulation and the role of emotional expression in symptom maintenance

title: Early Warning Signs of Bipolar Relapse: Investigating Critical Slowing Down in Smartphone Data
summary: This study examined whether smartphone-based digital phenotyping could detect early warning signals (increased autocorrelation and variance) before mood episodes in 29 bipolar disorder patients tracked over one year (10,587 patient-days). Activity-related measures predicted manic episodes, while sleep parameters predicted both manic and depressive transitions. However, even the best combined predictors did not meet predefined clinical utility thresholds on ROC analysis, indicating that while the dynamical systems approach shows promise, current predictive accuracy remains insufficient for clinical deployment.
clinical_insight: While digital phenotyping for bipolar disorder is not yet clinically actionable, this study demonstrates that passive smartphone data captures meaningful signal about impending episodes. Clinicians should be aware that patients increasingly use such tools and may bring self-tracked data into sessions. The honest reporting of below-threshold accuracy is methodologically commendable. The tension between research promise and clinical readiness remains unresolved—future work may benefit from combining digital phenotyping with clinician judgment rather than replacing it.
relevant_for: Clinicians managing bipolar disorder who are interested in digital mental health tools and relapse prevention strategies

title: Mental Imagery and Its Role in the Psychopathology and Treatment of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: A Systematic Review and Narrative Synthesis
summary: This systematic review examined the role of intrusive mental imagery in OCD psychopathology and treatment. Up to 89% of OCD patients reported experiencing intrusive imagery, often vivid and accompanied by bodily sensations, with 70-79% relating intrusions to adverse or traumatic events. Compared to other anxiety disorders, intrusive imagery in OCD occurred more frequently and often led patients to believe they possessed a ‘dangerous self.’ Imagery-based treatments including Imagery Rescripting and EMDR showed promise in reducing symptoms, offering an effective alternative for patients whose primary symptoms are imagery-based.
clinical_insight: This review provides strong justification for routinely assessing intrusive imagery in OCD patients, not just intrusive thoughts. The finding that most OCD imagery is trauma-linked suggests overlapping mechanisms with PTSD and supports trauma-informed approaches to OCD treatment. EMDR and Imagery Rescripting emerge as evidence-supported adjuncts for imagery-dominant OCD presentations. A blind spot: the review notes limited high-quality RCT data for imagery-based interventions in OCD, so while promising, these should be offered as complements rather than replacements for first-line treatments like ERP.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating OCD, especially those whose patients report vivid intrusive images or have comorbid trauma histories

title: Mapping Psychological (In)Flexibility in U.S. Military Veterans: A Directed Acyclic Graph Approach to the Hexaflex Model
summary: Using Directed Acyclic Graphs in a sample of 283 U.S. military veterans, this study mapped potential causal pathways between components of the ACT Hexaflex model. Committed action and acceptance emerged as central drivers of psychological flexibility, while cognitive fusion appeared to initiate cascades of inflexibility—flowing through self-as-content and inaction to experiential avoidance, diminished present-moment awareness, and lost values contact. The study provides the first DAG-based analysis of the Hexaflex in a veteran population at elevated risk for mental health complaints.
clinical_insight: The identification of cognitive fusion as the primary entry point into inflexibility cascades has clear clinical implications: targeting defusion early in ACT treatment for veterans may disrupt downstream dysfunction across multiple Hexaflex processes. The central role of committed action in flexibility pathways supports behavioral activation as a key mechanism of change. Clinicians should note that these are cross-sectional pathways, not confirmed causal mechanisms, though the DAG approach provides stronger causal inference than standard correlational analyses.
relevant_for: ACT practitioners working with veterans or trauma-exposed populations, particularly those interested in process-based therapy and mechanisms of change

title: Resilience in Sexual Assault Victimization: Do Mindfulness and Emotion Regulation Buffer Associations with Post-Traumatic Stress?
summary: This study compared post-traumatic stress severity across three victimization profiles in 247 cisgender women aged 21-30: childhood sexual abuse only, adulthood sexual assault only, and revictimized (both CSA and ASA). Revictimized participants showed the highest PTSS severity, followed by ASA-only and CSA-only. Mindfulness and emotion regulation were directly associated with reduced PTSS across all profiles, suggesting these psychological skills buffer trauma symptoms regardless of victimization timing or pattern.
clinical_insight: The finding that mindfulness and emotion regulation reduce PTSS across all victimization profiles—including the most severe revictimization group—supports incorporating these skills into trauma-focused treatments. For therapists, this reinforces the value of teaching mindfulness-based and emotion regulation strategies (e.g., DBT skills, ACT mindfulness) as transdiagnostic protective factors rather than as primary treatments. A limitation: the cross-sectional design cannot establish causation, and the sample was restricted to cisgender women aged 21-30, limiting generalizability to male survivors, older adults, or gender-diverse individuals.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with sexual trauma survivors, particularly those treating young adult women with complex victimization histories

title: Machine Learning Prediction of Treatment Response to Internet-Delivered Cognitive Behavioural Therapy vs. Antidepressant Medication
summary: This study developed and validated a multivariable predictive model for iCBT treatment response using real-world data from 776 patients. An elastic net regression with 27 predictors (including baseline depression severity, treatment expectation, and transdiagnostic symptoms) explained 14.6% of variance in depression change. A key finding was that models trained exclusively on iCBT patients showed treatment-specificity (R2=19.3% for iCBT vs. 10.8% for antidepressant-only), suggesting that treatment-specific models may better guide personalized treatment selection.
clinical_insight: This study supports the feasibility of using routine self-report data to guide treatment selection between iCBT and pharmacotherapy for depression. Clinicians should note that treatment expectation emerged as a key predictor, reinforcing the importance of expectancy management early in therapy. The finding that treatment-specific models outperformed mixed-treatment models has a notable blind spot: 24-34% of participants were concurrently receiving both treatments, potentially inflating cross-treatment generalizability. Nonetheless, this represents a concrete step toward personalized treatment matching in routine care.
relevant_for: Clinicians involved in treatment selection for depression, particularly those offering or referring to internet-based CBT programs

title: Cost-Utility Analysis of Internet-Delivered Cognitive Control Training Plus Treatment as Usual Versus Treatment as Usual Alone for Relapse Prevention in Major Depressive Disorder
summary: This health economic evaluation assessed the cost-utility of adding internet-delivered Cognitive Control Training (aPASAT) to treatment as usual for adults in remission from MDD. Using a Markov model over a five-year time horizon from the Belgian healthcare payer perspective, aPASAT + TAU dominated TAU alone, yielding additional QALYs and lower healthcare costs. Probabilistic sensitivity analysis indicated a 99.9% probability of cost-effectiveness at a €48,000/QALY threshold, with scenario analyses consistently confirming cost savings and improved outcomes.
clinical_insight: The finding that a fully unguided, low-cost digital intervention can both improve outcomes and reduce costs has significant implications for stepped-care models in depression relapse prevention. Clinicians working in primary care or mental health systems should consider recommending CCT as a scalable prevention tool for remitted MDD patients. A notable blind spot: the model parameters rely heavily on published literature rather than primary trial data for the economic evaluation, so real-world implementation may yield different cost profiles. The tension between the promise of scalable digital prevention and the need for long-term adherence data remains unresolved.
relevant_for: Clinicians and healthcare administrators designing relapse prevention pathways for major depressive disorder

title: Internet Cognitive Therapy for Prolonged Grief Disorder (iCT-PG): A Developmental Case Series
summary: This study evaluated a novel 12-session, therapist-assisted internet cognitive therapy adapted from iCT-PTSD for Prolonged Grief Disorder (iCT-PG). Eight adults meeting PGD criteria completed the program over 14 weeks, targeting loss-related memory characteristics, negative grief appraisals, social disconnection, and unhelpful coping. All participants completed treatment, with 87.5% showing both reliable improvement and recovery below clinical cut-offs. Large reductions were observed in comorbid PTSD, depression, anxiety, and functional impairment, maintained at 3-month follow-up. Average therapist time was approximately 10 hours per participant.
clinical_insight: The adaptation of evidence-based CT for PTSD to target PGD-specific maintenance mechanisms represents a promising approach for a disorder with limited treatment access. The strong recovery rate (87.5%) and low therapist time (10 hours) support the scalability of this format. Clinicians should note that the intervention explicitly targeted cognitive mechanisms (negative grief appraisals, memory characteristics) rather than solely providing supportive counseling. The primary blind spot is the uncontrolled design and tiny sample—these results cannot distinguish treatment effects from natural remission or therapist attention, though the consistency of improvement across multiple outcome domains is encouraging.
relevant_for: Grief therapists and clinicians working with prolonged grief disorder, particularly those seeking scalable digital treatment options

title: When “Bothered by” Means “Often”: Evidence for Misinterpretation of the Patient Health Questionnaire
summary: This study investigated how respondents interpret the PHQ’s instructions across a general population sample (N=503) and a clinical sample with elevated depression (N=349). Only 54.7% of community participants and 15.5% of the clinical sample interpreted the PHQ as instructed (frequency of being bothered). When asked directly, only 21.3% and 11.7% respectively had responded based on the instructed interpretation, with even fewer intending to do so in the future. PHQ-8 validity varied depending on how participants interpreted the instructions, raising questions about whether the measure captures symptom frequency, distress severity, or some combination.
clinical_insight: This study has immediate clinical implications: if only 11-21% of respondents interpret the PHQ as intended, then depression severity scores in both research and clinical practice are systematically contaminated by response interpretation variance. Clinicians should be aware that a patient reporting being “bothered a lot” may be reporting frequency rather than distress, or vice versa. The finding that clinical participants were even less likely to interpret instructions correctly than community participants suggests that severity of symptoms may further distort comprehension. A blind spot: the study cannot determine which interpretation produces more valid depression measurement—it only demonstrates that the current instrument conflates distinct constructs.
relevant_for: All clinicians using the PHQ-9, PHQ-8, or PHQ-2 for depression screening or outcome monitoring

title: Diminished Positive Affect in Social Anhedonia: Linking Anticipation and Experience During Dyadic Interaction
summary: This preregistered study examined how social anhedonia relates to affective responses before, during, and after dyadic social interactions in 100 young adults. Controlling for social anxiety and suspiciousness, social anhedonia was specifically associated with diminished positive affect both in anticipation of and during social interaction, but not with increased negative affect. Negative affect during interaction was specifically explained by co-occurring social anxiety. An indirect effect of social anhedonia via diminished positive experience on social motivation and volition was also found.
clinical_insight: The dissociation between social anhedonia (diminished positive affect) and social anxiety (increased negative affect) has direct implications for differential diagnosis and treatment planning. Clinicians often encounter patients presenting with social withdrawal who may have either or both conditions. This study suggests that treatments should be tailored: social anhedonia may respond to behavioral activation targeting reward processing and positive affect forecasting, whereas social anxiety requires exposure-based approaches. The exploratory finding that socially anhedonic participants had a comforting effect on their interaction partners warrants investigation. A limitation: the WEIRD young adult sample limits generalizability to older clinical populations.
relevant_for: Clinicians assessing and treating adults with social withdrawal, social anhedonia, or social anxiety disorder

title: Anxiety Identity Centrality Is Associated With Avoidant Coping in Anxious Adults
summary: In a large sample of 1,234 high-anxiety adults, this study examined whether anxiety identity centrality—seeing one’s anxiety as important to one’s self-concept—predicts avoidant coping. Using Bayesian ordered probit regression, anxiety identity centrality showed small-to-medium correlations with situational (r=.42) and experiential (r=.12) avoidance. Crucially, these associations held after controlling for anxiety symptom severity, suggesting that identity centrality contributes to avoidance beyond symptom level alone. The construct was measurable with a single-item ordinal measure.
clinical_insight: This study introduces a clinically relevant construct with direct therapeutic implications: patients who have incorporated anxiety into their identity may resist treatment approaches that could reduce their anxiety, as improvement would threaten their self-concept. Clinicians should consider assessing anxiety identity centrality early in treatment, particularly for patients who seem stuck in avoidant patterns despite adequate symptom-focused intervention. The debate about whether labeling mental health conditions promotes or hinders recovery is given empirical grounding here. A blind spot: the cross-sectional design cannot establish whether anxiety centrality causes avoidance or emerges from chronic anxiety experiences, limiting causal inferences for treatment planning.
relevant_for: CBT and ACT practitioners working with anxious adults who show chronic avoidant coping patterns

title: When Happiness Becomes Aversive: A Meta-Analysis of Fear of Happiness in Depression and Anxiety
summary: This meta-analysis examined associations between fear of happiness—a construct representing reward devaluation—and psychopathology in depression and anxiety. The analysis synthesized available evidence on how aversive responses to positive affect relate to symptom severity and treatment outcomes. The construct is particularly relevant because many current interventions target reward deficits in depression and anxiety, yet fear of happiness may undermine the efficacy of these reward-based approaches by making positive emotional experiences feel threatening or unsafe.
clinical_insight: Fear of happiness represents an underrecognized mechanism that may explain why some patients with depression or anxiety fail to benefit from interventions targeting positive affect or reward processing (e.g., behavioral activation, positive psychology interventions). Clinicians should be alert to patients who resist, avoid, or feel distressed by positive experiences, as this may indicate fear of happiness rather than simple anhedonia. This construct bridges the literatures on reward processing, emotion regulation, and clinical intervention. A key blind spot: the meta-analysis highlights the current lack of validated, brief clinical measures for fear of happiness, limiting its integration into routine assessment. The tension between treating reward deficits and inadvertently triggering reward devaluation remains a challenge for intervention design.
relevant_for: Clinicians using behavioral activation, positive psychology, or well-being interventions who encounter patients who resist positive experiences

title: Enhancing the Efficacy of Exposure Therapy: Translation of Pharmacological Augmentation of Fear Extinction
summary: This critical review examines translational research on pharmacological enhancement of fear extinction, focusing on D-cycloserine (DCS)—the most extensively studied agent. Despite early promise, results across preclinical, human laboratory, and clinical trials have been mixed. The authors identify factors accounting for inconsistent findings including study design differences, subject selection, sample size, and measurement approaches. They argue for a rigorous mechanistic research agenda that distinguishes between acquisition, consolidation, and retrieval of extinction processes as separate targets, and examines the relationship between changes in these processes and clinical outcomes.
clinical_insight: The mixed evidence on DCS augmentation of exposure therapy has left many clinicians uncertain about its utility. This review provides a framework for understanding why results have been inconsistent and offers a roadmap for future research. For practicing clinicians, the key takeaway is that pharmacological augmentation of exposure remains experimental rather than ready for routine clinical use. The debate over whether DCS enhances extinction learning, consolidation, or retrieval has direct implications for when and how it should be administered relative to exposure sessions. A significant blind spot: the review largely focuses on DCS, leaving other potentially promising augmentation agents (e.g., MDMA, psilocybin) outside its scope.
relevant_for: Exposure therapists and researchers interested in pharmacological augmentation strategies for anxiety disorder treatment

title: Guided Recall of Positive Autobiographical Memories Increases Anticipated Pleasure and Psychological Resources, and Reduces Depressive Symptoms: A Replication and Extension of a Randomized Controlled Trial of Brief Positive Cognitive-Reminiscence Therapy
summary: This RCT (N=75) replicated and extended findings on a three-session group-based positive-memory version of cognitive-reminiscence therapy (CRT). Relative to controls, the CRT group showed significantly higher anticipated pleasure (d=0.76-0.93), improved self-esteem, self-efficacy, and optimism (d=0.58-0.99), and reduced depressive symptoms (d=0.56-0.67) at post-treatment and follow-up. The intervention works partly through reducing automatic negative thoughts and enhancing awareness of narrative identity.
clinical_insight: CRT offers a brief, structured, and highly transportable intervention that directly targets the impoverished positive future-thinking seen in depression. Clinicians can deploy it as a standalone 3-session module or as an adjunctive ‘memory booster’ within CBT, schema therapy, or behavioral activation protocols. The anticipated pleasure findings are particularly noteworthy—they suggest CRT may directly remediate anhedonia, one of the most treatment-resistant depressive symptoms. A blind spot: the sample was predominantly female and the long-term durability beyond follow-up remains untested.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with adults experiencing depression, anhedonia, or low self-worth, especially in brief-therapy or primary care settings.

title: Trajectories of Depression Symptoms, Anxiety Symptoms and Functional Impairment During Internet-Enabled Cognitive-Behavioural Therapy
summary: Using growth mixture modelling on 52,029 iCBT patients, four trajectory classes were identified for depression (PHQ-9), anxiety (GAD-7), and functional impairment (WSAS): no change, gradual improvement, fast improvement, and a mild-baseline class. In moderate-to-severe baseline groups, fast improvers were more likely older, employed, without disability, and without OCD comorbidity. Functional impairment trajectories mirrored depression more closely than anxiety, underscoring its clinical importance as an outcome.
clinical_insight: This is the largest trajectory study in internet-enabled CBT to date and provides actionable baseline predictors for therapists: patients who are older, employed, and lack OCD comorbidity are most likely to improve rapidly. The identification of a ‘no change’ class—even among those receiving active treatment—highlights the need for early identification algorithms and stepped-care escalation. Functional impairment should be routinely tracked alongside symptom scales, as it captures a distinct dimension of recovery. A limitation is the absence of qualitative data explaining why the no-change group stalls.
relevant_for: Clinicians delivering iCBT or digital mental health interventions, and service designers building adaptive treatment algorithms.

title: Functional Correlates of Subliminal Stimulation in Posttraumatic Stress Disorder: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis (23 studies, 5 in fMRI meta-analysis) examined neuroimaging correlates of subliminal trauma-related stimulation in PTSD. The Innate Alarm System (IAS)—including the amygdala and related regions—showed hyperactivation in response to unconsciously processed threat cues. IAS response intensity scaled with PTSD severity: it was minimal in healthy controls and maximal in patients with dissociative symptoms or poor treatment responsiveness. Comparisons with other disorders (e.g., phobias) revealed contrasting patterns.
clinical_insight: The demonstration that the amygdala-based threat system activates to traumatic cues outside conscious awareness has profound clinical implications. It provides a neurobiological rationale for why PTSD patients experience seemingly ‘triggered’ responses without identifiable conscious antecedents, and why exposure-based therapies must address both explicit and implicit threat processing. The finding that dissociative patients show the strongest subliminal responses suggests that dissociation may represent a maladaptive top-down attempt to suppress an overactive IAS. Clinicians should consider integrating body-based or implicit processing interventions (e.g., EMDR, sensorimotor psychotherapy) alongside traditional talk therapies. A gap is the lack of longitudinal data tracking whether IAS responsivity normalizes with successful treatment.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with PTSD, complex trauma, and dissociation, especially those integrating somatic or neurobiologically-informed approaches.

title: Cognitive Dependencies and Psychological Health Correlates of Coherence in Autobiographical Reasoning
summary: This study (N=272, retest N=189) developed and validated a German version of the Awareness of Narrative Identity Questionnaire (ANIQ) measuring coherence in autobiographical reasoning. Coherence was independent of intelligence and verbal memory (partially dependent on figural memory), and was significantly associated with depression, positivity, self-esteem, and self-esteem stability—but not anxiety. The findings establish autobiographical coherence as a distinct psychological construct with salutogenic effects, particularly for mood-related outcomes.
clinical_insight: For therapists using narrative therapy, schema therapy, or identity-focused interventions, this study validates that helping clients build a coherent life narrative directly supports self-esteem and reduces depression—independent of cognitive ability. The dissociation from anxiety is clinically important: narrative coherence work may be more effective for depressive presentations than for anxiety disorders. The ANIQ offers a potential outcome measure for tracking progress in therapies targeting identity and self-concept. A limitation is the exclusively German sample and cross-sectional design, which cannot establish causation—depression may reduce narrative coherence rather than vice versa.
relevant_for: Clinicians using narrative therapy, schema therapy, or identity-based interventions with adults experiencing depression and low self-worth.

title: Cancer-Related Posttraumatic Growth and Posttraumatic Stress: Different Correlates and Different Network Structure in Patients with Cancer and Cancer Survivors
summary: This study compared PTSD and posttraumatic growth (PTG) in patients in active cancer treatment (N=181) versus cancer survivors (N=419), examining sociodemographic, cancer-related, coping, emotional, personality, eudaimonic, social, and spiritual correlates. Network analysis revealed that PTSD and PTG were linked in fundamentally different patterns between the two groups, with distinct central symptoms and bridge nodes. Results highlight which correlates matter most for each population—active patients versus survivors—and challenge one-size-fits-all approaches to posttraumatic adaptation in oncology.
clinical_insight: This study underscores that trauma-informed care for cancer patients must be phase-specific: interventions during active treatment should target different mechanisms than those for survivors. For therapists working in oncology or with medical trauma, the network approach offers a nuanced alternative to simple symptom checklists—it identifies which symptoms are ‘bridges’ between PTSD and growth, suggesting leverage points for intervention. The finding that different coping styles (e.g., anxious preoccupation vs. fighting spirit) serve as key correlates in each group can inform tailored CBT and ACT protocols. A gap is the cross-sectional design, which precludes causal inferences about whether PTG causes reduced PTSD or co-occurs independently.
relevant_for: Clinicians providing psycho-oncology support, trauma-informed therapy for medical illness populations, and those working with chronic illness adaptation.

title: Peritraumatic Reactions During Sexual Assault: Associations with Assault Characteristics and Mental Health
summary: In a cross-sectional study of 425 recent sexual assault victims (96% female, M=26.2 years), exploratory factor analysis of a 23-item peritraumatic reactions questionnaire developed with lived-experience experts identified six factors: Dissociation and freeze, Cognitive strategies, Active defense behavior, Negative emotions, Perceived life threat, and Reactive surrender. Dissociation/freeze and reactive surrender were far more common than active resistance, directly challenging rape stereotypes. All factors except cognitive strategies and active defense were positively associated with post-traumatic stress and depression/anxiety symptoms.
clinical_insight: This study provides clinicians with an empirically grounded framework for assessing peritraumatic responses that moves beyond simplistic fight-or-flight models. The identification of reactive surrender as a common, adaptive survival strategy directly challenges victim-blaming narratives that clients often internalize. For therapists conducting trauma processing (PE, EMDR, CPT), understanding which peritraumatic reactions most strongly predict later PTSD and depression can inform case conceptualization and treatment prioritization. A limitation is the cross-sectional design and female-heavy sample, which constrains generalizability to male victims.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with sexual assault survivors, particularly those using trauma-focused therapies such as PE, EMDR, or CPT.

title: Examining the Impact of a Brief Compassion Focused Intervention on Everyday Experiences of Compassion in Autistic Adults Through Psychophysiology and Experience Sampling
summary: This study explored a brief one-week online self-compassion exercise in autistic adults using self-report, experience sampling, and HRV. Trait self-compassion improved significantly pre- to post-intervention, as did fears of self-compassion, but changes did not extend to acute psychological distress or emotion regulation difficulties. HRV increased significantly during self-compassionate practice. Experience sampling revealed that acting self-compassionately predicted greater activation of drive and soothing affective systems and reduced threat system activation in the moment.
clinical_insight: This study provides multimodal evidence that brief online compassion exercises are feasible and physiologically meaningful for autistic adults, even when acute symptom change is not yet detectable. The experience sampling finding that momentary self-compassionate action directly engages soothing and drive systems while dampening threat response offers clinicians a concrete rationale for encouraging between-session practice. The dissociation between trait-level improvement and acute distress reduction suggests that longer or more intensive CFT protocols may be needed before symptom change emerges. A blind spot is the absence of a comparison control group and the relatively short intervention duration.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with autistic adults experiencing anxiety, depression, or self-criticism, particularly those integrating compassion-focused therapy approaches.

title: Emotion Regulation and Psychopathology: Investigating Differential Associations Between Emotion Regulation Skills and Psychological Symptoms Using a Network Approach
summary: Using routine clinical data from 716 psychotherapy outpatients, this network analysis examined differential associations between nine emotion regulation skills and nine symptom dimensions. Bridge expected influence analysis revealed that only three ER skills were most directly connected to symptom severity: Tolerance, Confrontation, and Modification. The strongest direct edges were Modification-Depression, Confrontation-Obsession-Compulsion, and Tolerance-Interpersonal Sensitivity.
clinical_insight: This study addresses a longstanding clinical question: which ER skills actually matter for which symptoms? The finding that Tolerance, Confrontation, and Modification serve as the primary bridges between ER deficits and psychopathology has direct treatment implications. For depression, targeting cognitive modification may be most impactful; for OCD, confrontational approaches to unwanted thoughts may be key; for interpersonal sensitivity, distress tolerance is paramount. This supports transdiagnostic protocols like the Unified Protocol while suggesting that personalizing which skills receive the most emphasis could improve outcomes. A limitation is the cross-sectional network, which cannot establish directionality.
relevant_for: Clinicians delivering transdiagnostic treatments (UP, ACT, DBT skills) who need to prioritize which emotion regulation skills to target based on presenting symptoms.

title: Broaden-and-Build Cycles of Attachment Security in Therapy: Antecedents, Consequences, and Trajectory of Therapeutic Exploration
summary: This study introduced the Client Therapeutic Exploration scales (CTE-4), a new measure of self-disclosure grounded in Bowlby attachment theory, scored on depth, emotional involvement, freedom, and spontaneity. Video-recorded therapy sessions from 36 patients across five time points were analyzed. Higher attachment anxiety consistently predicted less therapeutic exploration across all CTE-4 dimensions, while attachment avoidance was not significantly associated.
clinical_insight: The finding that attachment anxiety, but not avoidance, consistently constrains therapeutic exploration has nuanced implications for case formulation. Anxiously attached clients may need more explicit permission and safety-building before engaging in the exploratory work that drives change. The CTE-4 offers therapists a framework for monitoring the quality of client participation beyond session attendance. The tension between the theoretical model and mixed empirical findings highlights that therapeutic exploration is necessary but not sufficient for change. A gap is the small sample and the exploratory nature of the analysis.
relevant_for: Psychodynamic, attachment-based, and relational therapists seeking to understand how client attachment patterns shape the therapeutic process and depth of self-disclosure.

title: Anxiety as a Disorder of Uncertainty: Implications for Understanding Maladaptive Anxiety, Anxious Avoidance, and Exposure Therapy
summary: This paper integrates neurocomputational learning models with the clinical exposure therapy literature to propose that anxiety disorders are fundamentally disorders of uncertainty learning rather than exaggerated threat expectancies. The framework reconceptualizes exposure therapy as remediating maladaptive avoidance from dysfunctional explore/exploit decisions in uncertain situations, reconciling several inconsistencies in the literature including why exposure sometimes fails and why patients drop out.
clinical_insight: For clinicians delivering exposure-based treatments, this framework offers a conceptual shift: exposure works by restoring adaptive explore/exploit balance under uncertainty. This reframes common clinical challenges such as dropout, non-response, and habituation failure as predictable consequences of the patient uncertainty-learning architecture. It supports approaches that explicitly foreground uncertainty tolerance and suggests that exposure may be more effective when the explore/exploit framing is made explicit. A limitation is the absence of empirical data directly testing the framework.
relevant_for: Clinicians specializing in anxiety disorders and exposure therapy (CBT, ACT) who want a deeper theoretical understanding of why exposure works and when it fails.

title: Burst Versus Continuous Delivery Design in Digital Mental Health Interventions: Evidence from a Randomized Clinical Trial
summary: This RCT (N=93) compared burst delivery (five daily prompts in weeks 1 and 3 only) versus continuous delivery (five daily prompts throughout all three weeks) of ReApp, a mobile app promoting positive cognitive reappraisal. No significant differences emerged in adherence, depressive symptoms, anxiety symptoms, perceived stress, or perceived helpfulness. The burst delivery group showed significantly steeper decline in perceived difficulty over time. Both groups improved equivalently on clinical outcomes.
clinical_insight: This finding has practical significance for digital therapeutics design: delivering interventions in concentrated bursts with built-in breaks is as effective as continuous delivery while reducing perceived burden. For clinicians recommending digital adjuncts or step-down tools, this supports a less demanding approach that may improve long-term engagement and real-world adherence. The study specifically tested cognitive reappraisal, so generalizability to other therapeutic mechanisms remains uncertain. A gap is the small sample and the absence of follow-up data beyond the three-week intervention period.
relevant_for: Clinicians incorporating digital mental health tools and app-based interventions into their practice, particularly as adjuncts between sessions.

title: Dimensions of Skill Use in the Unified Protocol: Exploring Unique Effects on Anxiety and Depression
summary: Using data from the first-stage randomization of a SMART trial of the Unified Protocol (UP), this study examined whether patient skill use, defined by knowledge, frequency, quality, and effectiveness of UP skills, predicted within- and between-person changes in anxiety and depression. General skillfulness was tested as a predictor of symptom trajectories, aiming to establish which dimensions of skill engagement contribute most to therapeutic change in this transdiagnostic CBT-based protocol.
clinical_insight: The Unified Protocol is increasingly adopted as a transdiagnostic intervention, yet evidence on which specific skill dimensions drive change has been limited. By dissecting skillfulness into knowledge, frequency, quality, and effectiveness, this study can inform therapists about where to focus their efforts. If skill quality or effectiveness rather than mere frequency proves most predictive, it supports a deeper approach to skill coaching over homework compliance. This is relevant for therapists using UP, CBT-E, or any transdiagnostic protocol teaching multiple skills simultaneously. A blind spot is that the specific magnitude and direction of effects require reading the full paper.
relevant_for: Clinicians trained in the Unified Protocol or other transdiagnostic CBT approaches seeking to optimize how they teach and monitor emotion regulation skill use.

title: Instinctual Refinement Therapy for Complex Trauma: A Proof-of-Concept Case Series and Research Roadmap
summary: This proof-of-concept case series (N=30, 70% female, mean age 32.4) evaluated Instinctual Refinement Therapy (IRT), a novel protocol targeting evolutionarily conserved instinctual systems disrupted by early adverse experiences. IRT integrates predictive processing and memory reconsolidation frameworks. Preliminary results showed a 94% clinical improvement rate (28/30 cases) over a mean of 54.2 sessions, with large aggregate effect sizes (d=2.1) maintained at follow-up in 80% of assessed cases. Presentations were diverse and often treatment-resistant, including anxiety, mood, and psychotic disorders.
clinical_insight: IRT represents a potentially paradigm-shifting approach for complex, treatment-resistant trauma by targeting instinctual disruptions rather than cognitive schemas alone. The large effect sizes in a treatment-resistant population are striking, though the retrospective design and absence of a control group limit causal inference. Clinicians working with complex trauma clients who have not responded to conventional trauma-focused therapies (EMDR, TF-CBT, sensorimotor psychotherapy) may find IRT a promising next step. A critical blind spot is the lack of formal diagnostic characterization and the long mean treatment duration (54 sessions), which raises questions about feasibility in standard outpatient settings.
relevant_for: Clinicians specializing in complex trauma, treatment-resistant PTSD/CPTSD, and dissociation who are seeking novel therapeutic approaches for clients unresponsive to standard protocols.

title: Agency in Post-traumatic Stress Disorder: Implications for Therapy
summary: This paper proposes an enactive framework for understanding agency deficits in PTSD, linking peritraumatic dissociation to disrupted sense of control. The authors provide a roadmap for agency-based therapies and discuss how sensorimotor technologies such as gesture sonification could help restore agency during trauma recovery.
clinical_insight: The enactive perspective offers clinicians a principled way to understand why traditional exposure alone may fail when patients lack foundational agency. Sensorimotor approaches could complement phase-based trauma treatments, particularly for dissociative PTSD presentations. However, the framework remains theoretical and awaits empirical validation.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists working with dissociative PTSD and complex trauma seeking somatic adjuncts to phase-based treatment

title: Change of Threat Expectancy as Mechanism of Exposure Therapy: Evidence from 8484 Exercises of 605 Patients
summary: Using 8,484 exposure records from 605 patients across anxiety disorders, this study found that the learning rate (how effectively expectancy violation transfers into belief change) and expectancy change magnitude predicted treatment outcome, not violation alone.
clinical_insight: Clinicians should track whether threat beliefs actually update between sessions, not just whether patients complete exposures. Patients showing compliance without belief change may need cognitive restructuring or inhibitory learning strategies. A limitation is the naturalistic design without a control group.
relevant_for: CBT therapists conducting exposure-based treatment for anxiety disorders optimizing learning processes

title: Understanding CBT for Psychosis Through the Predictive Coding Framework
summary: This paper integrates CBTp with predictive coding from computational neuroscience, proposing that delusions persist through failed extinction learning and inappropriate reconsolidation of delusional priors, while CBTp works by strengthening non-delusional priors via behavioral experiments.
clinical_insight: Bridging CBTp with predictive coding gives therapists mechanistic language for why behavioral experiments work. The framework suggests spaced retrieval of non-delusional experiences may be more important than massed exposure. The theory remains empirically untested, which is a significant blind spot.
relevant_for: Psychologists and psychiatrists delivering CBTp who want deeper understanding of belief-update mechanisms

title: The Efficacy of Attentional Bias Modification for Anxiety: A Registered Replication
summary: This registered replication of Hazen et al. (2009) used a Bayesian approach to revisit ABM for GAD, also quantifying potential demand effects. Results add to growing evidence that ABM shows inconsistent efficacy for alleviating anxiety symptoms.
clinical_insight: This reinforces that clinicians should not divert patients from established treatments toward ABM as a standalone intervention. The debate now centers on whether ABM might work as an adjunct, which remains untested.
relevant_for: Clinicians and researchers interested in cognitive bias modification as a complement to standard anxiety treatments

title: Self-efficacy in Internet-based Mental Health Interventions: A Meta-analysis
summary: Meta-analysis of 70 RCTs (N=17,407) found moderate effects of internet-based interventions on self-efficacy, with guided interventions showing the largest effects. Self-efficacy emerged as a mediator of treatment outcomes, and lower baseline self-efficacy patients may benefit most.
clinical_insight: Supports explicitly targeting self-efficacy early in iCBT, particularly for patients with low confidence. The finding that lower self-efficacy patients benefit most is clinically important but counterintuitive. Lack of long-term follow-up on self-efficacy trajectories is a notable gap.
relevant_for: Clinicians delivering guided internet-based interventions who want to optimize treatment mechanisms

title: Single-Session Interventions for Mental Health: Umbrella Review
summary: First umbrella review of SSIs synthesizing 24 systematic reviews (415 unique trials) across anxiety, depression, externalizing, eating, and substance use problems. SSIs showed positive overall effect (SMD=-0.25), with 83% of reviews reporting significant effects.
clinical_insight: SSIs are a pragmatic tool for private practitioners to offer meaningful change in single contact, especially for waitlisted patients. Implementation research is the critical next step to determine which SSIs work in routine settings. The review does not address which formats work best for specific disorders.
relevant_for: Private practice clinicians and service managers maximizing impact of brief or single-visit interventions

title: Neuroception of Psychological Safety Scale (NPSS): Validation in UK Adults
summary: Validated the NPSS (3-factor measure rooted in polyvagal theory) in 2,035 UK adults. Social engagement, compassion, and body sensations subscales showed acceptable-to-excellent reliability, good test-retest, and predicted correlations with PTSD, burnout, and wellbeing.
clinical_insight: The NPSS helps trauma therapists assess capacity for psychological safety before beginning trauma processing. Measuring safety neuroception could determine whether a patient needs more stabilization. Performance in clinical trauma populations is still needed.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists using polyvagal-informed or phase-based approaches assessing readiness for trauma processing

title: Avatar-Based CBT/ACT Intervention for Female Orgasmic Disorder: RCT
summary: RCT (N=31) testing avatar-based CBT+ACT intervention in Second Life for female orgasmic disorder showed significant improvements across all outcomes with 100% intervention recovery vs. 0% control at follow-up.
clinical_insight: Virtual environments may reduce barriers to discussing sexuality. The CBT+ACT integration targeting both cognitive and experiential aspects is a strength. The 100% recovery rate raises demand-characteristic concerns warranting replication with larger samples.
relevant_for: Sex therapists and couples therapists working with female sexual dysfunction open to digital therapeutic tools

title: Anxiety and Compulsive Sexual Behaviour Disorder: Systematic Review
summary: Reviewed 36 quantitative studies on anxiety-CSBD relationship. Due to methodological heterogeneity, the relationship remained unclear, likely due to inconsistent CSBD measurement, limited gender diversity, and scarce longitudinal data.
clinical_insight: Clinicians should not assume anxiety drives compulsive sexual behavior or vice versa, but assess both independently. The review highlights that CSBD measurement remains problematic. Directionality cannot yet be determined.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with CSBD or hypersexuality needing to understand the anxiety comorbidity evidence base

title: Predicting Depression and Anxiety from Temporal Rumination Features
summary: ESM study (4 daily assessments, 2 weeks, N=40) found inertia of thought negativity predicted 67.2% of depression variance, outperforming trait rumination. Combined temporal and trait measures worked best. Moment-to-moment variability did not add predictive value.
clinical_insight: Inertia (stickiness) of negative thinking, not variability, predicts depression, supporting interventions targeting cognitive rigidity such as behavioral activation, cognitive defusion, or rumination postponement. Anxiety was better predicted by content features than temporal dynamics, suggesting different targets per disorder. Small sample warrants caution.
relevant_for: CBT and ACT therapists treating depression who want to refine rumination interventions by targeting cognitive inertia

title: Cognitive State Integration Method (CSIM): A Self-Administered Metacognitive Technique for State-Dependent Cognitive Changes in Obsessive-Compulsive Disorders
summary: Introduces the Cognitive State Integration Method (CSIM), a self-administered metacognitive technique for OCD that enables patients to record automatic thoughts and emotions during symptom episodes and compare them with baseline cognition during remission. Designed as an adjunct to Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP) within Reflective Cognitive Behavioral Training (RCBT), CSIM aims to enhance metacognitive awareness and facilitate recognition of intrusive thought inaccuracy.
clinical_insight: CSIM offers clinicians a structured tool to bridge the gap between in-session ERP work and patients' daily self-monitoring, potentially strengthening metacognitive awareness outside the therapy room. By providing a concrete comparison framework between symptomatic and remitted states, it may help patients develop more nuanced insight into the distorted nature of obsessional thinking. Clinicians working with treatment-resistant OCD patients may find CSIM particularly valuable as an adjunctive technique when standard ERP alone yields incomplete response.
relevant_for: Clinicians specializing in OCD treatment, particularly those integrating metacognitive and third-wave CBT approaches

title: Testing a Modular Online Self-Help Intervention in Adults with Clinical Perfectionism: A Randomized Controlled Trial
summary: A randomized controlled trial (N=114, mean age 33.9) testing a modular online self-help intervention for clinically significant perfectionism, allowing participants to select their own treatment modules. Participants were randomly assigned to an online training app or an active control condition (self-help book). Both interventions led to similar improvements over time and may serve as helpful low-intensity treatments for clinical perfectionism.
clinical_insight: This study provides preliminary evidence that client-driven modular approaches to perfectionism treatment may be feasible and acceptable, aligning with the broader shift toward personalized psychotherapy. For private practitioners, the modular online format could serve as a scalable between-session adjunct, enabling clients to work on perfectionism-related cognitions and behaviors independently. The active control comparison strengthens confidence that any observed effects are specific to the intervention. A limitation is the lack of a waitlist control condition.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with perfectionism, obsessive-compulsive presentations, and high-achieving adult clients in private practice

title: The Reciprocal Relationship Between Posttraumatic Stress Disorder Symptoms and Alcohol Use in a Large Multisite Longitudinal Sample Stratified by Sex
summary: A large multisite longitudinal study (n=2942, 62% female) examining bidirectional temporal relationships between PTSD symptoms and alcohol use over six months following trauma exposure, using random-intercept cross-lagged panel models stratified by sex. Males and females exhibit complex temporal development patterns aligning with the mutual maintenance hypothesis in men but the susceptibility hypothesis in women.
clinical_insight: The sex-stratified design is particularly valuable for clinicians, as it reveals differential pathways to comorbid PTSD-AUD presentations in men versus women, informing gender-sensitive treatment planning. Within-person fluctuations in PTSD symptoms predicted subsequent alcohol use between 3-6 months post-trauma, supporting targeted timing of interventions. The naturalistic longitudinal design across 29 emergency departments increases ecological validity.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating comorbid PTSD and alcohol use disorder, particularly those working in trauma-informed addiction services

title: Qualitative Analysis of a Patient's Experience of Ketamine-Assisted Psychotherapy for Substance Use Disorder: Empirical Synergies with Twelve-Step Programs
summary: A qualitative case analysis exploring a 28-year-old female patient's lived experience of ketamine-assisted psychotherapy (KAP) for alcohol and prescription opioid use disorder, examining empirical synergies with twelve-step program frameworks. The patient attributed psychological and spiritual changes to ketamine treatments, with experiences overlapping meaningfully with the 12 steps.
clinical_insight: As ketamine-assisted psychotherapy gains clinical traction for SUD, this qualitative work offers clinicians a framework for understanding patient experiences at the intersection of psychedelic therapy and twelve-step recovery. The identification of synergies between KAP and mutual-help frameworks is practically useful for clinicians navigating referrals between these modalities. However, the single-case design limits generalizability, and clinicians should be cautious about the dual-use potential of ketamine in SUD populations.
relevant_for: Clinicians integrating psychedelic-assisted therapies into addiction treatment, particularly those working with treatment-resistant SUD

title: Specific, Not Global, Disruptions in Attentional Ability Distinguish Risk for Suicide Ideation from Suicide Attempts: Results of a Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary: A systematic review and meta-analysis (12 studies, 26 effect sizes, N=995) examining neurocognitive attentional markers that differentiate individuals with suicidal ideation from those who have progressed to suicide attempts. People with SA history exhibited greater error rates yet more adaptive reaction time patterns compared to those with SI only, with task-specific differences across Stroop-like and Trail Making tasks.
clinical_insight: For clinicians conducting suicide risk assessments, this meta-analysis provides evidence that specific attentional deficits may be more precise markers of transition from ideation to action than broad cognitive impairment. This supports incorporating targeted attentional assessments into suicide risk formulation, potentially improving identification of high-risk patients. The findings also suggest attentional training interventions could be explored as a novel avenue for suicide prevention.
relevant_for: Clinicians conducting suicide risk assessments and those developing targeted cognitive interventions for suicide prevention

title: Adaptive Learning From Outcome Contingencies in Eating-Disorder Risk Groups
summary: Investigates adaptive learning from outcome contingencies in eating-disorder risk groups (n=82: recovered anorexia, high symptom, healthy controls) using a reinforcement learning task with eye-tracking. Unexpectedly, the recovered anorexia group showed elevated learning rate adjustment compared to controls, with reduced pupil dilation to volatile rewards, suggesting a complex relationship between eating disorders and cognitive inflexibility involving noradrenergic mechanisms.
clinical_insight: The reinforcement learning framework offers clinicians a mechanistic understanding of why patients with eating disorders struggle to update maladaptive beliefs about food, weight, and shape despite contradictory evidence. The unexpected finding of elevated learning rate adjustment in recovered anorexia suggests cognitive inflexibility in eating disorders may be state-dependent rather than trait-like, informing treatment approaches that explicitly target belief updating. The noradrenergic finding opens potential pharmacological adjunct avenues.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating eating disorders, particularly those working with patients who show limited response to standard CBT-E

title: The Perceived Causal Relations Between Sensory Reactivity Differences and Anxiety Symptoms in Autistic Adults
summary: Investigates how 246 autistic adults perceive causal relationships between sensory reactivity differences and anxiety symptoms. Sensory hyperreactivity (especially visual, auditory, olfactory) was perceived as causing anxiety, while sensory seeking (especially tactile, vestibular) was perceived as resulting from anxiety, suggesting different clinical pathways for intervention.
clinical_insight: This study is directly relevant for therapists working with autistic adults with co-occurring anxiety. Understanding patients' own causal models of how sensory experiences trigger anxiety can inform individualized treatment planning, particularly in differentiating between sensory-driven anxiety versus anxiety arising from other sources. The finding that sensory seeking may be an effect rather than cause of anxiety suggests different intervention targets.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with autistic adults, particularly those addressing co-occurring anxiety in neurodiversity-affirming practice

title: When Deteriorators Report Improvement in Psychotherapy: A Qualitative Analysis
summary: A qualitative analysis of psychotherapy clients who deteriorated on the OQ-45 but reported subjective improvement (paradoxical outcomes). Of 106 deteriorators, 58.5% believed they had improved. Interviews revealed increased insight, learned coping strategies, interpersonal improvements, and symptom reduction. Discrepancy explanations included extratherapeutic factors, changes in questionnaire interaction, undetected progress, and hindsight bias.
clinical_insight: This study has significant implications for routine outcome monitoring (ROM) programs where deteriorating scores trigger clinical alarms. The paradoxical outcome phenomenon suggests some patients experience meaningful subjective improvement even as standardized measures indicate worsening, potentially reflecting increased awareness or therapeutic deepening. Clinicians should interpret deteriorating OQ-45 scores with nuance, exploring the patient's subjective experience before adjusting treatment.
relevant_for: Clinicians using routine outcome monitoring and those supervising trainees in counseling center settings

title: Personalizing Chronic Pain Treatment Using Person-Specific Networks: A Single-Case Experimental Design Study
authors: Saskia Scholten, Verena Eunike Hofmann, Felicitas Kininger, Julia Glombiewski
summary: This proof-of-concept study evaluated whether person-specific network analysis can guide individualized treatment target selection for chronic pain patients with comorbidities. Twelve patients (mean age 60.25) and nine therapists participated in an ABCD single-case experimental design with randomized baselines. Qualitative interviews explored feasibility and acceptability. Compliance reached at least 80% in seven participants, with a mean of 298 observations per person. Multilevel analysis showed an average decrease in disability scores from baseline to intervention phase. The authors conclude that the clinical value of data-informed personalization lies not in algorithmic precision but in facilitating patient-centered, reflective clinical reasoning in complex cases.
clinical_insight: For therapists working with chronic pain, this study supports using repeated self-monitoring data to collaboratively identify each patient's unique symptom network before selecting intervention targets. Rather than applying a standard protocol, the approach uses individual network visualization to help patients recognize patterns in their own daily experience, then matches intervention modules to the most central nodes in their personal network. The finding that structured data-informed reflection, rather than algorithmic output per se, drove therapeutic benefit suggests that network-informed case formulation can be integrated into existing clinical workflows without requiring specialized software. Therapists treating complex somatic presentations where comorbidities obscure the primary maintaining mechanism may find this approach particularly useful for identifying which target to address first.
relevant_for: Therapists working with chronic pain and medically unexplained symptoms who want to integrate personalized formulation into treatment planning

title: The Interplay Between Cognitive-Behavioural and -Interpersonal Factors in Disordered Eating: A Network Analysis
authors: Chantal P Delaquis, Yasmin Brown, Dean Spirou, Jayanthi (Jay) Raman, Sylvie Berthoz
summary: This study integrated the transdiagnostic cognitive-behavioral and cognitive-interpersonal models of eating disorders using network analysis in 493 Australian participants reporting ED symptoms, of whom 271 scored above the clinical cutoff on the EDE-QS and 108 reported a lifetime eating disorder diagnosis. A partial correlation network was estimated, with bootstrapped stability and centrality difference tests. Concern Over Mistakes emerged as the most central node overall (expected influence = 1.48) and the strongest bridge node connecting cognitive-behavioral and interpersonal clusters (bridge expected influence = 0.86). The edge between Overvaluation of Weight and Shape and Weight and Shape Rigidity was among the strongest in the network (0.45). The network demonstrated excellent stability.
clinical_insight: The prominence of Concern Over Mistakes as both the most central symptom and the primary bridge between cognitive-behavioral and interpersonal dimensions suggests that perfectionism, rather than body dissatisfaction alone, may be the engine that connects disordered eating cognition with relational dysfunction. For therapists, this supports directly assessing and targeting perfectionism early in treatment, even when a client presents primarily with shape concerns or interpersonal difficulties. The strong connection between Overvaluation of Weight and Shape and Rigidity indicates that rigid beliefs about weight, rather than the content of those beliefs alone, may be a key maintaining factor that deserves explicit attention in cognitive restructuring. These findings are cross-sectional and cannot confirm causal pathways, but they provide a empirically grounded map for prioritizing treatment targets within the transdiagnostic framework.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating eating disorders using CBT-E or integrative approaches, particularly when clients present with comorbid perfectionism and interpersonal difficulties

title: "It's a balancing act": Autistic fathers navigating parenting in everyday life
authors: Chris Edwards, Abigail Love, Ru Ying Cai, Kees-jan Archbald, Dean Dickson, Sean Hynes, Abel Trindade, Sarah Edwards, Bronwyn Leigh, Vicki Gibbs
summary: This co-produced qualitative study explored the parenting experiences of 19 Autistic fathers using semi-structured interviews (spoken or written, per participant preference), analyzed with reflexive thematic analysis. Four Autistic fathers collaborated as co-researchers in study design, interview development, and analysis. Five themes were generated: finding joy and meaning in everyday fatherhood moments; becoming oneself through fatherhood, where parenting strengthened Autistic identity; walking the tightrope of family life, reflecting the continuous effort of balancing sensory, emotional, and practical demands; parenting as relational work, including the tension arising when fathers felt misunderstood by partners and family; and parenting in systems stacked against us, highlighting barriers in services and wider society that fail to recognize Autistic needs.
clinical_insight: Autistic fathers in this study described fatherhood as a source of identity consolidation and emotional connection, challenging the deficit-focused narrative that still predominates in clinical work with neurodivergent adults. The theme of walking a tightrope of sensory, emotional, and practical demands highlights that autistic parents may experience parenthood not as a category of impairment but as an ongoing balancing act that can become overwhelming when environmental demands exceed capacity. The finding that fathers felt most distressed not by parenting itself but by being misunderstood within family relationships and by services suggests that clinicians working with neurodivergent families should attend to the relational context and systemic barriers rather than focusing solely on individual coping strategies. When an autistic father presents in therapy, asking about parenting experiences may open a productive avenue for exploring identity, self-worth, and systemic stressors.
relevant_for: Therapists working with neurodivergent adults, particularly autistic parents or those navigating family life and relationship dynamics

title: "Supporting Children on Therapy Waitlists: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Web-Based Parent-Focused Single Session Intervention for Child Anxiety"
authors: Madelaine Abel, Jessica L. Schleider, Jenna Sung, Alayna D'Amico, Dina R. Hirshfeld-Becker
summary: This pilot parallel-group RCT evaluated a self-guided, web-based single-session intervention (SSI) called Project EMPOWER, delivered to parents of children ages 4-13 with anxiety or OCD who were on a waitlist at a hospital-based CBT clinic. The SSI targets parental accommodation, a well-established family factor that can worsen child anxiety and undermine CBT efficacy. Parents (N=47) were randomized to receive the SSI (N=24) or waitlist as usual (N=23). At 2-week follow-up, parents who received the SSI reported significant reductions in accommodation (d=0.59), child anxiety symptoms (d=1.25), and significant increases in parent agency (d=0.68). Effects were maintained until children started CBT (mean wait of 13.54 weeks). Parents rated the SSI as highly acceptable per preregistered benchmarks.
clinical_insight: Single-session interventions for parents offer a practical, scalable solution to one of the most frustrating systemic problems in child mental health: long waitlists that leave families without support for months. For clinicians running waitlisted services, this study provides evidence that a brief, self-guided web module targeting parental accommodation can meaningfully reduce both accommodation behaviors and child anxiety symptoms while families wait for formal therapy. The effect size on child anxiety (d=1.25) is strikingly large for such a minimal intervention, though the small sample size warrants caution. The focus on parental accommodation is well-chosen: it is one of the most modifiable and clinically impactful factors in child anxiety, and reducing it before CBT begins may actually improve subsequent treatment outcomes. Clinicians should consider recommending SSI resources to families on their waitlists, and those in private practice can use the core principles (psychoeducation about accommodation, guided exercises to reduce it) as take-home materials in the very first session.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with child anxiety in community or hospital settings, particularly those managing long waitlists, and therapists interested in single-session intervention approaches

title: "Targeting coping strategies, emotion regulation, and self-esteem in children with dyslexia to support mental health: A randomised-controlled trial of Clever Kids"
authors: Mark Boyes, Kirsten Melville, Mandy Nayton, Hannah Pizzey, Suze Leitao
summary: This pre-registered RCT evaluated Clever Kids, a nine-week socio-emotional wellbeing programme developed specifically for upper primary school children with dyslexia. Seventy-eight children (mean age 10.19 years, 54% female) with clinically diagnosed dyslexia were randomized to the programme (n=38) or a wait-list control (n=40). Outcomes included coping skills, emotion regulation, self-esteem, perseverance, assertiveness, and internalising and externalising symptoms measured at pre-, post-, and three-month follow-up. Significant intervention-by-time interactions were found for six outcomes: child-reported coping, emotion regulation, academic and social self-esteem, and parent-reported anxiety and depression, all maintained at three-month follow-up. The programme focuses on building coping strategies and emotional skills within a peer group of children who share the experience of dyslexia.
clinical_insight: Children with learning difficulties such as dyslexia are frequently overlooked in mental health provision, yet they experience elevated rates of anxiety, depression, and low self-esteem that often persist into adolescence and adulthood. This study demonstrates that a targeted, nine-week group programme can produce meaningful improvements across multiple domains of functioning. For therapists who work with children or young people, the findings underscore the importance of screening for learning difficulties when a child presents with anxiety or low mood, as the two are closely intertwined and the learning difficulty may be the primary driver of the emotional difficulty. Rather than treating the anxiety in isolation, referring to or replicating structured programmes like Clever Kids that address coping, emotion regulation, and self-esteem in the context of the child's specific learning profile may be more effective. The group format also leverages the normalizing power of shared experience among children with dyslexia, which can itself be therapeutic.
relevant_for: Therapists working with children and young people who have co-occurring learning difficulties and mental health needs, particularly in educational or school-based settings

title: "A randomized controlled trial exploring dose-response effects of mindfulness meditation practice on wellbeing"
authors: Nicholas I. Bowles, Nicholas T Van Dam, Jonathan N Davies, Julieta Galante
summary: This well-powered RCT (N=716 meditation-naive adults) examined dose-response effects of daily mindfulness meditation across four assigned durations (4, 10, 20, or 30 minutes per day) over a 28-day online intervention. Primary and secondary outcomes included wellbeing, mindfulness-related constructs, and mental health symptoms. All groups showed significant improvements across outcomes (SMD = 0.29-0.56), but no dose-response effect was found: longer assigned durations did not produce greater benefits. Adherence declined steeply at higher doses, with only 48.5% completing two-thirds of sessions in the 30-minute group compared to 70.9% in the 10-minute group. In exploratory analyses, actual practice engagement, goal confidence, and time predicted outcomes regardless of assigned dose.
clinical_insight: These findings have direct implications for how therapists recommend mindfulness to clients. The common clinical assumption that more practice time equals more benefit is not supported here: within the first 30 days, 4-10 minutes of daily practice produced benefits comparable to 30 minutes, while higher doses led to poorer adherence. For clients who struggle to maintain a meditation practice, therapists can confidently recommend short sessions (5-10 minutes) without worrying that they are offering an insufficient dose. This is particularly relevant for clients with depression or anxiety who may find extended sitting meditation aversive or overwhelming. The strong role of goal confidence in predicting outcomes also suggests that therapists should invest time in building clients' belief that mindfulness will help them, rather than focusing on the duration of practice. The study population was non-clinical, so extrapolation to clinical groups requires caution, but the adherence finding is likely even more important in clinical populations where motivation is often lower.
relevant_for: Therapists who incorporate mindfulness into their clinical work, whether as a standalone intervention or as part of broader protocols such as MBCT, ACT, or DBT

title: "A re-analysis of Chiang et al. (2017): Cognitive-behaviour therapy does not have a statistically significant effect on levels of depression or mania in bipolar disorder"
authors: David Smailes, Milo Johns, Peter Moseley
summary: This re-analysis challenges the widely cited Chiang et al. (2017) meta-analysis that concluded CBT is effective for reducing depression and mania in bipolar disorder. Smailes and colleagues identified that Chiang et al.'s conclusions depended partially on a pair of implausibly large effect sizes extracted from a 2010 RCT, and that a second publication of the same RCT reported much more plausible (and smaller) effect sizes. Replacing the implausible values with the corrected ones eliminates the statistically significant effect of CBT on both depression and mania severity. The original meta-analysis has been cited over 200 times and has influenced treatment guidelines.
clinical_insight: This re-analysis is important for any therapist who works with bipolar disorder and has used the Chiang et al. findings to justify CBT as an adjunctive treatment. While CBT for bipolar disorder is widely recommended in clinical guidelines, the evidence base may be weaker than previously believed.
